calendar.matters:
MOTION FOR ATTORNEY FEES AND EXPENSES
Off Calendar Aug-04-2025 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. Martin Glickfeld, Esq. of Law Offices of Martin Glickfeld (tel: 415-441-7491) for Plaintiff Sausalito Accessories Inc.
Atty. Cari Cohorn, Esq. of Workplace Legal, APLC (tel: 415-851-8900) for Defendant Xiao Mei Jin.

Hearing Re: RESPONDENT XIAO JIN's MOTION FOR ATTORNEY FEES AND EXPENSES.

Matter argued and submitted. Upon conclusion, the Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Respondent Xiaomei Jin's motion for an award of attorneys' fees of $89,304 (including a 1.5 multiplier) and costs in the amount of $1,263.37 is granted.

Appellant Sausalito Accessories, Inc. appealed an order of the Labor Commissioner and later withdrew its appeal. Jin now seeks fees, including a lodestar multiplier, and costs as the prevailing party on appeal.

Labor Code section 98.2, subdivision (c), states, "If the party seeking review by filing an appeal to the superior court is unsuccessful in the appeal, the court shall determine the costs and reasonable attorney's fees incurred by the other parties to the appeal, and assess that amount as a cost upon the party filing the appeal. An employee is successful if the court awards an amount greater than zero." "This statute is not a prevailing party fee provision, instead it is a one-way fee-shifting scheme that penalizes an unsuccessful party who appeals the commissioner's decision. Its purpose is to act as a disincentive to appeal the commissioner's decision and to discourage unmeritorious appeals of wage claims, thereby reducing the costs and delays of prolonged disputes, by imposing the full costs of litigation on the unsuccessful appellant." (Nishiki v. Danko Meredith, APC (2018) 25 Cal.App.5th 883, 894 [internal citations, quotation marks, and brackets omitted].)

Jin is the successful party in this action. (Arneson v. Royal Pacific Funding Corp. (2015) 239 Cal.App.4th 1275, 1280 ["The reading of section 98.2 which would preclude fees to a substantively successful responding employee-successful because the employer threw in the towel just before the fight-not only fails the test of legislative intent, it fails textually as well."].) The court concludes that the amount of fees and costs she seeks is reasonable using the lodestar method of a reasonable number of hours times a reasonable billing rate. Sausalito's opposition takes issue with the number of hours billed, but the contemporaneous billing records document counsel's activities and the court finds them reasonable and supported. Sausalito further contends that Jin should not recover fees for theories on which she might not have succeeded on appeal (such as by adding new claims). This logic was rejected in Nishiki. The court further finds that the hourly rates sought by Jin's counsel are reasonable in light of counsel's experience and background in this legal community.

Jin seeks a 1.5 multiplier of the lodestar. She was largely the prevailing party at the Labor Commissioner hearing and the definition of a successful employee on appeal is one who receives more than nothing. That cuts against a multiplier. But the risks of taking the case on a contingency basis and collecting a fee award cut in her favor. The court awards the requested 1.5 multiplier here; Sausalito's opposition brief offers no argument concerning the lodestar request.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. da Fonseca; Court reporter: Shelli Eng, CSR# 11397 (tel: 410-912-0456 / email: shellieng@gmail.com). (301/CVA)
